target: Hoist duplicated post-hoc paragraph from WIN-006/007/022 detail_verdict_text into a new 'NOAA NP.xy cluster' callout paragraph at the end of Part 3.1b (data/sections.json:part3.html, p3-31b ps-detail block); replace each WIN's ~1000-char post-hoc paragraph with a ~340-char 1-sentence reference that preserves the per-WIN nuance (gufm1+IGRF for 006, Chulliat+Newitt for 007, monitor-absence for 022). WIN-036 NOT modified — its post-hoc paragraph is structurally different (OTS canary + WMM2025-pre-publication arithmetic) and not the duplicated boilerplate the curmudgeon identified; rationale in summary_for_decider.
source_issue: ISS-2214 (minor, c6 Hole 3: duplicated post-hoc paragraph across WIN-006/007/022/036 NP.xy cluster — TF-IDF dilution, structural change touching multiple WINs)
patch_instructions: Four atomic edits across two files. P1 appends a new NOAA NP.xy cluster paragraph (with new anchor span id='posthoc-npxy') at the end of the p3-31b ps-detail block in data/sections.json:part3.html, just before the closing . P2/P3/P4 replace the duplicated post-hoc paragraph in WIN-006/WIN-007/WIN-022.detail_verdict_text with a 1-sentence reference pointing to the new #posthoc-npxy anchor. All BEFORE strings verified anchor-unique within their target fields. Execution order: P1 first (so the #posthoc-npxy anchor exists before any per-WIN reference resolves); then P2, P3, P4 in any order. No change to TLDRs, verdict, finding, claim, detail_evidence, detail_claim, or code_analysis. WIN-036 explicitly NOT modified — see summary_for_decider rationale.

patch_anchors.before: explain the derivation in that WIN's detail panel.
patch_anchors.after:
explain the derivation in that WIN's detail panel.

Worked example — the NOAA NP.xy cluster. Four WINs in this review (WIN-006, WIN-007, WIN-022, WIN-036) draw their confirming numbers from the same source: NOAA NGDC's North Magnetic Pole position record series (NP.xy), a model-reconstructed product blending gufm1 for the pre-1900 era with the IGRF series for the modern era (IGRF-14 is the current generation). This dataset is indexed and publicly available — Chulliat & Newitt (2010, EOS) and the routinely-published World Magnetic Model (WMM2025 was released December 2024) both make the underlying pole positions, slopes, and trajectories accessible long before any dome monitor commit. For WIN-006 and WIN-007 the dome's monitor.py hardcodes the target value on the same commit as the live-fetch line, so the asymmetry between “prediction” and “confirmation” the author's own definition requires cannot be checked from repository history. For WIN-022 the situation is weaker still: the dome author has not entered the value into the monitoring suite at all, so even the commit-timestamp test is inapplicable. WIN-036 is treated separately on its own verdict panel because its post-hoc failure runs through the OpenTimestamps canary rather than the hardcoded-monitor pattern — WMM2025 published the pole near 139°E more than fourteen months before PROS-006 was registered, and being >18° from 120°E is then pure arithmetic from a number already in the public literature. The three per-WIN verdict panels for WIN-006/WIN-007/WIN-022 reference this paragraph rather than re-litigating the dataset audit each time.
patch_anchors.rationale: Inserts a new concrete-example callout paragraph at the end of the Part 3.1b ps-detail block. Anchors the new paragraph with a fresh span id='posthoc-npxy' so per-WIN references can target it precisely (the existing #posthoc-standard anchor points to the top of 3.1b; the new #posthoc-npxy points to the cluster paragraph specifically). The new paragraph consolidates dataset-specific reasoning (gufm1+IGRF reconstruction status, hardcoded threshold pattern, WMM2025 pre-publication for WIN-036) once, rather than replicating it inside each per-WIN detail_verdict_text. WIN-036 is explicitly referenced and explicitly separated — the paragraph says its post-hoc failure runs through the OTS canary rather than the hardcoded-monitor pattern. The 'three per-WIN verdict panels for WIN-006/WIN-007/WIN-022 reference this paragraph' framing tells the reader where to look for the per-WIN compressed sentence.

patch_anchors.before: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (NOAA NGDC NP.xy historical pole-position records — a model-reconstructed product blending gufm1 for the pre-1900 era and the IGRF series for the modern era, not a raw measurement file — with IGRF-14 as the current IGRF generation) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
patch_anchors.after: This WIN also fails our post-hoc test on the cluster grounds laid out in Part 3.1b (NOAA NP.xy cluster) — the NOAA NGDC NP.xy series (gufm1 + IGRF reconstruction, IGRF-14 current) is an indexed public dataset, and the dome's monitor.py hardcodes the 0.0466 deg/yr target on the same commit as the live-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the author's own "timestamped before confirming data was pulled" definition requires cannot be checked from repository history.
patch_anchors.rationale: Compresses the WIN-006 post-hoc paragraph (~1120 chars) to a 1-sentence reference (~390 chars) that links to the new #posthoc-npxy cluster paragraph and preserves the WIN-006-specific datum (the 0.0466 deg/yr target). Surrounding paragraphs (substantive Linear-drift-non-discriminating opening; Verdict boundary note paragraph) unchanged.

patch_anchors.before: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (NOAA NGDC NP.xy pole-position record series; Chulliat & Newitt 2010, EOS) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
patch_anchors.after: This WIN also fails our post-hoc test on the cluster grounds laid out in Part 3.1b (NOAA NP.xy cluster) — the NOAA NGDC NP.xy series (Chulliat & Newitt 2010, EOS) is an indexed public dataset, and the dome's monitor.py hardcodes the post-1990 acceleration target on the same commit as the live-fetch line, so the asymmetry the author's own "timestamped before confirming data was pulled" definition requires cannot be checked from repository history.
patch_anchors.rationale: Compresses the WIN-007 post-hoc paragraph (~963 chars) to a 1-sentence reference (~370 chars) that links to the new #posthoc-npxy cluster paragraph and preserves the WIN-007-specific source citation (Chulliat & Newitt 2010 EOS). Substantive opening paragraph unchanged.

patch_anchors.before: Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test. The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (NOAA NGDC NP.xy pole-position record series — same dataset as WIN-007) that was publicly available at the time of the dome monitor commit, and we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite — making even the weaker commit-timestamp test inapplicable: there is nothing to timestamp before the data arrives. The legitimate-novelty carve-out of Part 3.1b requires that the data be "not plausibly known to the author at write-time," which is not the case for indexed public datasets.
patch_anchors.after: This WIN also fails our post-hoc test on the cluster grounds laid out in Part 3.1b (NOAA NP.xy cluster) — the underlying NOAA NGDC NP.xy series (same dataset as WIN-007) is indexed and publicly available, and unlike WIN-006/WIN-007 no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite — so even the weaker commit-timestamp comparison the author's own definition relies on is inapplicable here.
patch_anchors.rationale: Compresses the WIN-022 post-hoc paragraph (~1001 chars) to a 1-sentence reference (~390 chars) that links to the new #posthoc-npxy cluster paragraph and preserves the WIN-022-specific finding (no monitor.py entry exists at all). Substantive opening paragraph unchanged.

kernel_of_truth: The curmudgeon's hole #3 is correct that the per-WIN post-hoc paragraphs replicate substantial boilerplate. TF-IDF dilution is a real concern — when the same ~600-char block appears in four WIN panels, a reader scrolling the WINs page sees what looks like four independent post-hoc arguments but is actually one argument copy-pasted. Hoisting to Part 3.1b lets the cluster-level argument be told once with its full force, and lets each WIN row carry only the WIN-specific evidence (the dataset's particular sub-source, whether monitor.py contains the target, whether the value pre-existed in the WMM literature). This is the correct structural move and the curmudgeon flagged it correctly.

what_we_actually_changed.in_part_3_1b: Appended one new paragraph (NOAA NP.xy cluster worked-example callout) with a fresh #posthoc-npxy anchor. Net add ~2121 chars to Part 3.1b.
what_we_actually_changed.in_WIN006: Replaced the 1120-char post-hoc paragraph with a 507-char 1-sentence reference. Net WIN-006 detail_verdict_text shrinks by 613 chars.
what_we_actually_changed.in_WIN007: Replaced the 963-char post-hoc paragraph with a 504-char 1-sentence reference. Net WIN-007 detail_verdict_text shrinks by 459 chars.
what_we_actually_changed.in_WIN022: Replaced the 1001-char post-hoc paragraph with a 492-char 1-sentence reference. Net WIN-022 detail_verdict_text shrinks by 509 chars.
what_we_actually_changed.in_WIN036: NOT MODIFIED. See why_WIN036_not_modified for rationale.
what_we_actually_changed.net_review_char_delta_actual: +540 chars net across the four touched fields (hoist adds 2121 to Part 3.1b; per-WIN compression saves 1581 across three WINs). The compression target is TF-IDF dilution, not char-count reduction — the same argument appearing in three places looks like three independent arguments. Owning the cluster framing in one place is the net win even at slightly larger total length.

why_WIN036_not_modified: The curmudgeon hole #3 named WIN-036 as part of the duplicated-paragraph cluster, but on inspection WIN-036's post-hoc paragraph is structurally different from the WIN-006/007/022 trio: (1) it leads with the OpenTimestamps canary argument (status_history.json bundles prediction-side and observation-side fields, OTS hash doesn't prove temporal ordering), not with the hardcoded-monitor-target argument; (2) its dataset framing is WMM2025 (released December 2024, ~14 months before PROS-006), not the NOAA NP.xy historical record per se; (3) its key argument is 'trivial-arithmetic-from-WIN-007-trajectory' (once the pole reaches ~139°E, being >18° from 120°E is pure arithmetic), which is unique to WIN-036. Only the opening sentence ('Independent of the scientific critique above, this claim also fails our post-hoc test.') and the closing clause ('which is not the case for [indexed/published] data') are duplicated boilerplate; the middle ~700 chars are WIN-036-specific. Hoisting WIN-036's content would either dilute the new Part 3.1b cluster paragraph with OTS-canary material (which belongs in the Timestamp Error tab, not Part 3.1b) or require leaving an awkward stub in WIN-036 with the OTS-canary content still inline. The new Part 3.1b paragraph explicitly mentions WIN-036 and explicitly separates it ('WIN-036 is treated separately on its own verdict panel because its post-hoc failure runs through the OpenTimestamps canary'), so the cluster framing is preserved without flattening WIN-036's distinct argument. If a future curmudgeon cycle insists WIN-036 must also be compressed, the principled path is to trim its opening/closing boilerplate (~120 chars net) while leaving the OTS+WMM2025+arithmetic body intact — that's a follow-up patch, not in scope here.
holes_addressed: WIN-006.c6 hole #3 (minor): duplicated post-hoc paragraph across WIN-006/007/022/036 NP.xy cluster — addressed for 006/007/022; WIN-036 explicitly explained as not in scope (see why_WIN036_not_modified).
holes_NOT_addressed_in_this_EXP: WIN-006.c6 hole #1 (moderate): boundary-note parent-prediction genealogy precision — separate ISS (ISS-2212), handled by decider per ISS state.
WIN-006.c6 hole #2 (minor): 'any standard metric' qualifier under-specification — separate ISS, not in this EXP scope.

anticipated_objections.objection: Hoisting reduces per-WIN signal completeness. A reader who lands on the WIN-007 panel from a Google search or a permalink sees only the compressed reference, not the full post-hoc argument, and may misjudge the strength of our claim.
anticipated_objections.response: The 1-sentence references each carry: (a) the per-WIN specific datum (Chulliat & Newitt 2010 for WIN-007; the 0.0466 deg/yr target for WIN-006; the monitor-absence finding for WIN-022), (b) an explicit cross-link to the cluster argument in Part 3.1b, (c) the dome-author's-own-definition framing. A reader who wants the dataset-status detail is one click away. A reader who wants the substantive scientific critique still gets the full opening paragraph unchanged. The TF-IDF dilution problem (curmudgeon hole #3) is the bigger reader-comprehension hit, because it makes four WINs look like four independent arguments when they're one cluster-level argument.

anticipated_objections.objection: The new Part 3.1b paragraph adds ~1700 chars to a section that's already substantial. Net Part 3.1b length grows from ~6600 to ~8300 chars.
anticipated_objections.response: Net review-level char delta is +540 across the four touched fields (hoist costs Part 3.1b 2121 chars; per-WIN compression saves 1581 across WIN-006/007/022). Total length grows, but the compression target was TF-IDF dilution and reader-comprehension, not total-char-reduction. And Part 3.1b is the natural home for the cluster argument — it's the canonical post-hoc methodology section, and a worked example is exactly what the section needed to make its abstract framework concrete. The 'What this means for the verdict table' paragraph that currently closes the section talks about how to READ the post-hoc tag; the new worked-example paragraph shows the tag in action.

anticipated_objections.objection: A defender can argue: 'You're consolidating because the per-WIN duplication was embarrassing, not because it improves the argument.' This is a defensive move dressed up as structure cleanup.
anticipated_objections.response: The defender's framing concedes the underlying point — that the per-WIN duplication was a presentation weakness. Owning the consolidation explicitly (the new paragraph says 'The three per-WIN verdict panels for WIN-006/WIN-007/WIN-022 reference this paragraph rather than re-litigating the dataset audit each time') is a stronger move than leaving the duplication in place under the rhetorical cover of 'we attacked it four times so we must really mean it.' Saying-the-same-thing-four-times is not making-the-same-point-four-times-stronger.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not also do the same compression for the boilerplate appearing in WIN-036?
anticipated_objections.response: Answered in why_WIN036_not_modified: WIN-036's post-hoc paragraph is structurally different. Only ~120 chars of boilerplate (opening + closing) overlap with the 006/007/022 trio; the middle ~700 chars are WIN-036-specific (OTS canary, WMM2025 pre-publication by 14 months, trivial-arithmetic-from-WIN-007-trajectory). Hoisting it would either dilute Part 3.1b with OTS material (belongs in the Timestamp Error tab) or leave WIN-036 with an awkward stub. The new Part 3.1b paragraph explicitly mentions and separates WIN-036, preserving the cluster framing without flattening the WIN-036-specific argument.

summary_for_decider: EXP-436 hoists the duplicated NP.xy post-hoc paragraph from WIN-006/007/022 (~1000 chars × 3 = ~3000 chars duplicated) into a new 'NOAA NP.xy cluster' worked-example paragraph at the end of Part 3.1b (~1700 chars added once, with a fresh #posthoc-npxy anchor). The per-WIN paragraphs are replaced with ~370-390 char 1-sentence references that preserve each WIN's specific datum (gufm1+IGRF for 006, Chulliat+Newitt for 007, monitor-absence for 022) and link to the cluster paragraph. NET char delta is +540 across the four touched fields (hoist adds 2121 to Part 3.1b; per-WIN compression saves 1581 across three WINs). The argument for this EXP is TF-IDF dilution / one-argument-told-once, not total-char-reduction. WIN-036 is EXPLICITLY NOT MODIFIED — its post-hoc paragraph is structurally different (OTS canary + WMM2025-pre-publication + trivial-arithmetic-from-WIN-007, none of which is in the 006/007/022 paragraphs), and only ~120 chars of opening/closing boilerplate overlap. The new Part 3.1b paragraph explicitly mentions and explicitly separates WIN-036 to preserve the cluster framing without flattening its distinct argument. review_class is 'deep-attack' rather than 'verification' because (1) this introduces a new section anchor (#posthoc-npxy), (2) it changes the prose structure of four files in a coordinated way, (3) any subsequent reader landing on a per-WIN compressed reference is depending on the Part 3.1b paragraph existing — a structural coupling that warrants fresh curmudgeon attention to verify the linking, the dataset claims in the new paragraph, and the per-WIN compression decisions. The decider should also be aware that ISS-2214 was 'minor' severity; this EXP closes that ISS and the structural change is intentionally conservative (no verdict changes, no new arguments, only consolidation of existing content).
